explanation: The sentence essentially says: 'At prop_0, if prop_1, then prop_2'. This is a conditional: whenever prop_0 and prop_1 are true at a moment, prop_2 also must be true at that moment. Globally (always), at any point, if prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 must also hold.

The natural language says: In PSV Mode, at the condition 'prop_0', if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_2' must also hold (referring to CONT.41). This can be translated as: always, whenever both prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 must also hold. This is a safety property.
The statement means that always, if prop_0 holds, and if prop_1 holds, then prop_2 must hold. This is best expressed as: always, (if prop_0 & prop_1 then prop_2).

The specification seems to indicate that at the end of every inspiration phase, if property 'prop_0' holds, then property 'prop_1' must also hold at that same moment. Assuming 'at the end of inspiration phase' can be mapped to some recurring moment (which is modeled by the atomic proposition 'prop_0'), then 'prop_1' must be true whenever 'prop_0' is true. This can be captured by the formula: Globally, whenever 'prop_0', then 'prop_1'.

The sentence says that at the end of an inspiration phase, a certain property holds if BOTH prop_0 AND prop_1. Since the condition focuses on 'if prop_0 and prop_1', we express that whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) is true at the end of an inspiration phase, then some consequent should follow. Since there is no consequent provided in this fragment, the formal way to capture 'at the end of inspiration phase, if prop_0 and prop_1' would be to mark the occurrence where both become true. But as only the condition is mentioned without a required action, and considering the absence of negation or requirement, the literal LTL translation of the condition is simply 'prop_0 & prop_1'.
The statement says that at 'prop_0', if 'prop_1', then 'prop_2'. This can be interpreted as: whenever 'prop_0' holds, if 'prop_1' also holds, then 'prop_2' must hold as well.

The phrase can be interpreted as: At any point where prop_0 holds, if prop_1 holds at the same time, then prop_2 must also hold at that moment. In LTL, this is usually captured by saying 'globally, whenever prop_0 and prop_1 hold, then prop_2 holds.'

The specification says: 'At the prop_0, if the prop_1, an prop_2 (see CONT.41).' Interpreting this, it suggests that whenever prop_0 holds, if prop_1 holds at the same time, then prop_2 must also hold. In LTL, this is naturally written as a global constraint: whenever (prop_0 & prop_1) holds, prop_2 must also hold. This can be captured by G((prop_0 & prop_1) -> prop_2).

The requirement states that at state 'prop_0', if 'prop_1' holds, then 'prop_2' should also hold. This can be understood as: whenever 'prop_0' and 'prop_1' are both true, 'prop_2' must also be true. This must hold globally throughout the execution.

The statement can be interpreted as: when prop_0 holds, and if prop_1 holds, then prop_2 should also hold. This translates to: globally, whenever prop_0 and prop_1 both hold, then prop_2 also holds at that time.
